text: The transabdominal view reveals the deep inguinal ring, vas deferens, and spermatic vessels. The lateral and medial umbilical folds are visible alongside the inferior epigastric vessels. The hernia defect is observed lateral to the epigastric vessels and medial to the deep ring.

The surgeon fast-forwards through the dissection, revealing the hernia and preperitoneal fat. The pseudosac and transversalis fascia are teased away from the distal aspect of the preperitoneal fat. The surgeon identifies a lateral direct hernia and proceeds with careful dissection, focusing on medial and lateral areas. Tissue is pushed to the lateral side wall in preparation for mesh placement, while parietalization of the vas and vessels is performed.

The surgeon returns to the preperitoneal fat hernia, with the epigastric vessels visible medially. The preperitoneal fat is nearly fully reduced, revealing the iliopubic tract and peritoneal edge. Henle's ligament and Hasselbeck's ligament are identified, confirming a direct hernia lateral to the epigastric vessels. The spermatic vessels and vas deferens are seen entering the canal independently, supported by the transversalis sling. The hernia is reduced, and the surgeon continues to develop an adequate pocket.

The surgeon uses their left hand to grasp the obliterated umbilical artery, a consistent anatomical landmark, while the vas deferens and peritoneal edge are visible. Electrocautery is used cautiously to further dissect around these structures, emphasizing the identification of the obliterated umbilical artery to define the extent of the dissection.
